narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 47% probability that you would fail it. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, including academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and enrollment plans, to help the school admit those most likely to succeed. For you, as a Black male with a lower-middle family income background, the model highlighted three key factors that shaped this outcome, with your LSAT score having the strongest pull toward predicting failure. Your LSAT score of 20.7, well below the typical 36.7 average, stood out as the biggest concern, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to most applicants. Next in influence was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far lower than the average of 5.53, which suggested inconsistent academic performance in later undergraduate years and further tipped the scales toward failure. On a more positive note, your family income bracket actually worked in your favor, pushing the prediction slightly toward passing since students from similar backgrounds have sometimes outperformed expectations. Being in the small minority of part-time enrollees (just 7.3% of students) didn't sway things much here, nor did your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 or Year 1 decile of 3, both below averages like 3.21 and 5.71. Overall, these top influences meant your profile aligned more closely with students who faced bar exam challenges than those who passed easily. While this is disappointing, especially given the hurdles many Black students like yourself navigate in competitive fields, it doesn't define your potential. To strengthen future applications, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, improving late-stage grades if possible, and gathering evidence of your resilience, such as work experience or extracurriculars that demonstrate bar readiness. Consider targeted prep programs tailored for underrepresented students, and reapply after addressing these gaps—many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort.
